transcript.text: Hi, Ryan.
Hi, Andi.
How are you doing?
Good.
How are you?
I'm doing well.
Thank you.
Thanks for chatting with me on a Friday afternoon.
Yeah.
Are you East Coast or West Coast?
East Coast.
Okay.
Yeah.
Sorry about that.
This was one of the available times I had.
Yeah.
All good.
Where are you located?
I'm in Brooklyn.
Oh, okay.
Nice.
So East Coast as well.
Yeah.
Okay.
Awesome.
Cool.

Well, I can give you a little overview on the role, talk a little bit about GrowthX and kind of what we're looking for, and then we can talk about you, and then I'll try and leave some time at the end for questions.
Sure.
Which we should be able to do, shouldn't be too hard.
Cool.
So, GrowthX is really a, we're an agency first, we take an agency first approach to like the startup world, I guess.
We are a content development agency, strictly content, we don't do, like no paid, no like social, like we're not doing anything else, but content development.
And we, all of our content is generated using our own proprietary platform that leverages AI to
For workflows that generate content.
I said content generation a lot.
It is Friday afternoon.
But the, so basically the way that we think about this, the way that what we found is, we have pods that are working on writing articles and working with specific clients.
We have like 40 clients right now.
And we're seeing like people have a lot of experience in at marketing agencies in content, like journalism, SEO experience, things like that.
But I think we've just started to find that, you know, when you work with customers over a long period of time, you kind of get stuck in the trees.
And it's hard to like take a step back, do like a full, like second level of SEO audit and change strategy.
Like you kind of get, not in a
Right, but like a little bit on a path and it's hard to change lanes or shift strategies when you're like working day to day with a client and so close to them.

So what we're looking for here is kind of like an IC floater who can take a high level approach to evaluating like progress, looking at analytics and doing an audit on an occasional rolling basis with every client over a period of time and saying, okay, it's been like maybe three months since we've changed up the strategy.

Here's what's working, here's what's not, here's a new direction and just helping like pop in, change the lens a little bit so that we can keep making sure that we're delivering for every customer and like showing results and making sure that we're staying like relevant and part of the conversation.
That also means like thinking a lot about GEO right now and making sure that people are showing up with LLMs.
There's also like a very, there's like an experimentation component to this too, because we haven't worked, like nobody knows what works for GEO, right?

We have best practices, we have like, but we're trying, you know, we're trying to start to track like where we see movement with like some of the, some experiments that we're running internally and kind of what we can recommend and what we should roll out to all of our clients, things like that.

And so I think somebody that can have that holistic view across all clients and is just thinking about like SEO and performance and like looking for patterns at a high level will help us be effective, like on the ground with every customer.
So that's the role.
I know it took me a little while to get it out, but any questions?
Yeah.
I mean, just right.
Off the bat, I've been tracking GEO and progress with, you know, AI and LLMs and, you know, people are freaking out about it on LinkedIn with their posts.

But you did mention that, you know, there hasn't been, for lack of a better phrasing, a bona fide way to test it and see what does and doesn't work because we don't know what the algorithms of LLMs are.
But you did say that y'all are sort of being mindful and paying attention.
Is there anything in particular that GrowthX is doing right now to track progress or results with sites that are appearing in those AI searches?
So we are running, like, you know, for customers that are really curious and want to try some things, like we're definitely leveraging LLMs TXT files for, like, site mapping.
And we've, we have a machine learning.
Data analyst or something.
She has like a crazy title.
She was just consulting, so I haven't met her.
But she's like starting to compile kind of like end track long term.
We've only started doing that in the last month because I think it's only been like available in the last month for a lot of clients.
So that's one experiment.
We also have seen some clients that are like trying to enter the conversation and like Reddit and Discord and things like that.

And so we want to see if like those results start to actually be referenced places because prevailing knowledge is that social media, you know, that LLMs are crawling social media for like objective takes on things.
And then like we've also seen like you can install a button on a page that says like summarize with chat GPT.
amazing.
we've as
Um, I don't know if we're tracking that one, but I know like some clients are playing around with something like that.
Right.
So, and then like, we're, we're also trying to track, we're doing a lot of refreshing right now, just content refreshing.
Um, and so we're trying to pay attention to like, is that really moving the needle?
I mean, refresh content always like, it's always useful.
Um, but you know, we'd like to be able to have some predictability and to like how effective it is.
Yeah.
Um, yeah, totally agree.
Um, I think one of my approaches or sort of ways of, of looking at it, I guess, uh, on that holistic level you mentioned was to reinforce brand authority versus domain authority.
So it's not to suggest we should ignore domain authority, but from what I've read and what I've learned about, you know, everything going on.
these LLMs are, uh,
Attributing citations, the same way search engines would attribute backlinks.
So the more your brand or company is mentioned, then the more likely it is to appear in those AI results.
So not really quite sure where I was going with that.
But I think that's sort of what, you know, Warren's mentioning and bringing up, like, that's sort of one of the, I guess, KPIs that I've seen emerge.
Um, when you say you're looking for someone to come in and do more top level, uh, does that mean, you know, aside from audits, are we talking in keyword research?
Like, is this what, is this what the services at growthx?
Are we, are we talking?
we do, like, we, our content managers are mostly doing, like, keyword research and, and like, you know, topic clusters, things like that.
Like, um, I think that's, we can easily do.
at a base level.
But there is a secondary, you know, layer to that of, yeah, that like, site audit gap analysis, we do do like a keyword gap analysis.
But I think that there's a more sophisticated approach that we can take.

And again, it just gets really easy to kind of get a little single tracked if you're working with a customer day in, day out, and you and like, you develop a rapport, and then you get a little lazy, it's you know, it's easy to get into a pattern.
And so that's why we're looking for somebody that can help shake things up on a ongoing rolling basis.
Yeah, sure.
And I'm assuming technical SEO would be a huge, like aspect of the role.
Yes.
Okay, gotcha.
Yeah, so I spun by the site, I guess what I was trying to wrap my head around is like, does growth,
Does have its own proprietary dashboard or is it just, you know, we're going to create a workflow and then institute it into the strategy for a client?
Is that more what it is?
We have our own platform that we're using internally to generate content.
So, and that is like, it's the platform runs workflows.
And for every client and every client has kind of a workspace where we have myriad workflows, depending on what we're trying to accomplish.
And then the, the dashboard right now we're using scrunch.
I think we've heard like profound is very good for tracking that sort of thing.
But right now we're just using scrunch.
It seems like it's a pretty easy thing to like build or replicate anyways.
So who's to say that?
scrunches forever.
And then we right now use Looker in terms of tracking performance.
So we have access to all of our clients, GA4, GSC consoles, but that will come in-house and be attached to our proprietary platform as well.
Gotcha.
And SEMrush or Ahrefs?
SEMrush right now.
I think we're not married to that, but SEMrush right now.
And is the role a client-facing one?
Am I...

So I think, like, there are options or degrees to which that could, like, depending on who we hire, like, you could either serve up a strategy, talk the director through it, and have them present it, or, like, you know, you can join the call.
But I think I'm agnostic on that at the moment.
Okay.
Yeah, that sounds great.
sounds like there's a lot of room for the person who steps in to kind of define the role as that person moves through it.
Yeah.
Well, so we spent a lot of time.
We got to ask questions up front.
So why don't we get to you?
Okay.
Yeah.
Yeah.
We only have 15 minutes left.
So, yeah.
Why don't you tell me a little bit about yourself?
That's a habit of mine, by the way.
I know they're supposed to be safe for the end, but I always jump the gun.
Yeah.
Yes.
I'm happy to tell you a little bit about me.
I started doing SEO back in 2016 as I was sort of pivoting from a copywriting career.
A company out in Austin called Volusion, which is a shopping cart software company who was looking to compete with Shopify.
I think we know who won that race, was establishing their own in-house marketing department.
Sorry.
There you go.
Thank
So SEO was sort of new, and I was sort of new, and they took a chance on me.
And I learned a lot about the ins and outs of e-commerce and how to provide SEO for that particular space.
I had clients that really were across the spectrum.
I had one client that sold light bulbs.
I had another client that sold nuts, literally nuts and bolts for like, you know, like those peach tree crane lift machine things.
Yeah.

I think the biggest challenge that I overcame over there was I, two of my clients, one was in nutritional supplements, and the other was in vaping supplies and accessories, which are two industries where you're not allowed to offer paid advertising.
So, yeah, I had to get very creative and had to really lean into expertise or lean into my sort of evaluation of the organic space.
And how could I make those clients stand apart?
So naturally, you know, various student keyword research and building out their on-page content so that it ran the gamut and kind of serviced search user intent.
But there was also a lot of product page schema instituted.
There was a lot of FAQ schema instituted throughout those.
And I really got those clients out in front and saw traffic significantly increase.
I guess in addition to, you know, key metrics like form submissions and email clicks as well.
From there, I moved back to New York.
That's where I'm from.
So got a little homesick, took a job at a place called ThomasNet.
Similar setup, but they didn't necessarily sell a product.
They were a, for lack of a better description, they were a directory for manufacturers to connect with suppliers.
So, you know, small example is, you know, the refrigerator in your kitchen right now, that appliance is built by three or four different companies, not just one.
And so the handle for the refrigerator is made by a client that would sign up with ThomasNet.
Um, so I was working with a lot of, um, clients that service the very niche audience.
Um, and I was doing, you know, the same on and off page tactics, uh, but with a bit more of a slant towards demand and lead gen tactics, uh, and some AB testing thrown in there as well.

Um, uh, in one instance, I had a client, uh, that was looking to sort of stop the, they were, they were losing traffic from, um, homepage slash landing page to their catalog pages, which were on a sub domain.
Um, and essentially what I did was, uh, sort of ran the site through the usual tech SEO auditing software.
Screaming Frog, to kind of analyze crawl depth and internal linking and how effective it was.

And what we essentially did was, again, a lot of the same tactics in terms of instituting schema on their catalog pages and canonicalizing a lot of their categories so that search engines would understand one page to represent a number of pages for a number of products.
And then got a little creative in terms of on-page visual assets.
A good example is created an interactive infographic of a clean room.
That's what this client sort of catered to.
And throughout the illustrated clean room, we had vibrating bullet points that identified the different products in the clean room or different aspects.
And if a user hovered over it, they'd be able to click right to the product page.
So we just sort of like, you
You know, shorten the roadmap that a user had to take to get to a product page.
And we did see sales significantly increase.
This was before the pandemic, when, of course, we saw sales dramatically increase.
Everybody was looking for rubber gloves and their products.
Also had a client that offered metal stamping services.
And in that scenario, what they were looking to do was compete with more local businesses.
In the Chicago area.
So in that instance, tailored a lot of my strategy to off-page tactics, boosted, or rather, optimized their Google business profile, leveraged that to find long tail and query-based keywords.
And then just did a blanket sort of optimization of the website so that they would appear in more Chicago-based searches.
What did you do there?
Like, how did you Like, how did
How you do that?
How did you, like, what did you find to make them more localized?
Well, at the time, because in my next role was where that became a little bit more defined.

So I'll just mention that since then, and in my next role working at Home Builders, I found that on SEMrush, there is a feature where you could heat map geographical areas that your business is located in.
And it will show you the most sort of searched for keywords, such as near me, best blah, blah, in New Orleans.
And then you leverage that data to create content that services that EET agenda, but that also helps your clients stand apart in that crowd.
That combined with off-page strategies, such as...

As the Google business profile, we would add, obviously, we would ensure NAP is uniform across all profiles, but we would also utilize the posting feature on Google business profiles, which I don't know that a lot of people do, at least not SEO strategists.
Some of those had a shelf life, because Google, I believe at the time, only gave you a week to post and then let it sit there.
I think it's designed more for like announcements and sales, but we just stayed on top of it, you know, two or three posts every few months.
We cross posted with social channels, like LinkedIn, or Facebook or Pinterest, whatever the client wanted.
And then hit up some of those local directories, so that we'd get that backlink juice coming as well.
All of that combined with a more hyper-localized content strategy equated to the results that...
You know, we saw, which was, you know, higher visibility, better keyword ranking for local keyword searches, et cetera, et cetera.
Yeah, I'm running a little bit.
I apologize.
No, that's okay.
I'm just super conscious of our time because I do have a hard stop in eight minutes.
So talk to me about like a time when you had conflicting requests from different clients and like how you prioritize and think about prioritizing across clients.
Yeah, mean, I know this is going to be a cop-out answer, but it really is a case-by-case basis.
You know, I've been in marketing for almost a decade.
I know that money talks.
If an enterprise client needs something yesterday, the enterprise client gets it yesterday.
If it's a matter of a hard deadline, which an SEO is rare, then the priority is the hard deadline.
That's usually a technical issue if a site is completely offline.
That now becomes the priority.
So, you know, again, I hate to provide such a vague...
No, that's fine.
Yeah.
Can you give me an example of a time that you, like, adapted, had to adapt quickly to changing client needs?
A time I had to adapt quickly.
I mean, I have a very long roll of the decks of clients, and I'm sure that that issue came up frequently.
I think the blanket response for this, because it has surely happened, you know, often in my career.
Yeah, I mean, I think in those instances, I would assess the needs and what can be completed and how we can shift priorities from our side.
But obviously, articulating and walking through whatever hazards or obstacles might be in the way of that.
I guess the best example I could think of is a client insisted on using their own branded language for their products and their services.
But as you and I know, that's...
That's not how people search for things.
So it was really a matter of explaining that to them.
It's a matter of compromising and saying, I can use your branded language, but just so you're aware, this is what people are searching for.
There is the search volume and the keyword difficulty and all the data that you need to see to understand how to get your website in front of these people.
But we could surely use your branded language.
It just means that we have to, you know, shapeshift the content strategy a little bit.
Yeah.
How do you think about like short-term wins versus like long-term strategy for SEO?
I mean, SEO by its nature is a long-term game.
The short-term wins for me are, you know, are we passing the website through audit software and addressing issues that can be taken care of right away?
Am I able to articulate and collaborate with developers?
developers?

partners?

Long-term wins are in the hands of me, the client, and search engines.
Yeah.
LLMs now.
Yeah, and LLMs.
How do you think about, like, presenting your strategy to clients, and especially non-technical clients?
How do you think about speaking their language and convincing them of what you want to do?
I mean, I'm very comfortable speaking with clients.
I've had training outside of my professional life to assist with that.
So, very comfortable with creating slide decks and presenting to clients.
What was the...
What was the...
the second part of the question.
Well, just like, yeah, how do you think about, like, the non-technical, for non-technical clients, like, how do you think about convincing them of the strategy?
So less like the method of, well, could be the method of presentation, but, you know, how do you think about that?
I think a lot of that entails me walking them through specifically what SEO is designed to do and how it aligns with their needs.

Whenever I'm speaking with a client, whether it's during onboarding or in the middle or towards the end of a year of a campaign, I like to use the two-audience analogy with them, and that helps them understand SEO much greater than they might have before.
And that analogy is essentially a website is being seen by two audiences.
There's the human audience that's looking at everything that's in front of your face and on a website, but then there's the search engine audience.
There's, which is looking at all the code, you know, I like to click view page.
source and show them all that, you know, crazy, kooky HTML.
And that helps them understand that the strategy involves servicing both of those audiences.
So we want to make sure that the human audience is satisfied because they're finding what they need in a relatively quick amount of time.
But we also want to satisfy the search engine audience by including all the bells and whistles required to make sure that that site is being evaluated properly.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Cool.

And especially, like, thinking about, I mean, we're growing super fast, so scalability and kind of just making sure that other people know how to do something if they need to, things like that.

so she created like a, you know, a quick guide or a loom.

Yeah.

But, yeah, individually created status reports and then instituting that template so people can follow along also kind of helps.
